results.possibleIdentity: Harley Jones is a British filmmaker, film editor, and educator who was active from the early 1960s through at least the 1980s. He began his career as a film editor for the BBC, working on programs such as John Grierson's weekly Scottish Television series 'This Wonderful World' from 1963 to 1966. In 1966, he founded and became the first course leader of the Newport Film Unit at the Newport College of Art and Design in Wales, where he taught until 1982. Jones was known for creating experimental and impressionistic works, such as the film 'Resurrection,' an homage to steam engines. He also directed documentaries, including 'You and Your Health' (1979). In the late 1960s and 1970s, he was involved with the Arts Council of Great Britain, serving on committees for the Visual Arts and Film and contributing to the development of experimental film and independent production in the UK. His background in both technical film editing and experimental art education aligns with the multidisciplinary mission of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization.

Harley Jones (born 1953) is a New Zealand-born virtuoso concert accordionist, teacher, and digital entrepreneur who gained prominence in the late 1960s. He won the New Zealand Accordion Championship at age 14 and represented New Zealand at the Coupe Mondiale International Accordion Solo Competition in New York in 1969. Active throughout the 1970s, he recorded an L.P. with his brother Maurice in 1975 and performed as a soloist with various orchestras, including the New Zealand Symphony Orchestra. In 1996, he founded 'Accordions Worldwide,' a major online resource for the accordion community. While primarily known as a musician, his presence in the New York art and music scene during the height of the E.A.T. movement (1969) and his later focus on the intersection of music and digital technology reflect the collaborative spirit often associated with the organization.

results.possibleIdentity: Marcial Rodriguez (sometimes spelled Marceal) was a Spanish artist and collaborator active in the mid-20th century, particularly noted for his influence on literature and theater. He is famously cited by author Peter S. Beagle as the inspiration for the 1968 fantasy novel 'The Last Unicorn'; Beagle received a painting from Rodriguez in 1956 depicting unicorns fighting bulls, which served as a primary creative catalyst. In the late 1950s and 1960s, a Marcial Rodriguez (specifically Marcial Rodriguez Fernández) was associated with the Spanish Republican exile community in New York and was involved in theatrical productions at Barnard College and Columbia University, such as a 1958 production of 'La vida es sueño'. This historical identity aligns with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) era, an organization that promoted collaborations between artists and engineers in New York during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Furthermore, archival snippets from 1979 link a 'Marcial Rodriguez' to pioneering work in computer technology and engineering, reinforcing a potential technical or collaborative artistic role typical of the E.A.T. circle.

results.possibleIdentity: Agnes Denes (born May 1931, Budapest, Hungary) is a prominent Hungarian-American artist recognized as a pioneer of conceptual, environmental, and ecological art. Moving to Sweden as a teenager and later to the United States in the 1950s, she studied at the New School and Columbia University in New York. She became active in the late 1960s, a period during which she shifted from painting to a unique 'visual philosophy' that integrated science, mathematics, linguistics, and philosophy. Denes was an early participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization; archival records from 1969 include her membership forms, project proposals, and photographs within E.A.T.'s Technical Services Program, which matched artists with engineers. Her early career in the late 1960s and 1970s is marked by landmark works such as 'Rice/Tree/Burial' (1968), regarded as one of the first large-scale site-specific ecological sculptures, and the 'Philosophical Drawings' series (1969–1980). She is also well known for her 'Pyramid Series' (begun 1970) and 'Map Projections' (begun 1973), which reinterpreted geography and geometry. An early adopter of technology, she experimented with computers in the early 1970s and created some of the first hologram artworks and X-ray images of flora and fauna. While her most famous work, 'Wheatfield — A Confrontation', occurred in 1982, her foundational influence was established through her multidisciplinary and ecological projects of the 1960s and 70s.

While primarily an engineer, Purdie is frequently associated with artistic and cultural projects from this era due to the visual and performative nature of the steam excursions he managed. Purdie was an 'honorary member' of the Brotherhood of Locomotive Engineers and Trainmen and is considered a pioneer and icon of the steam locomotive preservation movement, which emerged in tandem with organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in the mid-1960s.

Bernard "Pretty" Purdie (born 1939) is a world-renowned American drummer and artist who was highly active in the late 1960s and 1970s. Often referred to as "Bill" or "Bernard Purdie," he is known for his influential work in R&B, soul, and funk, including the creation of the "Purdie Shuffle." results.possibleIdentity: Andrew Stratford is an English creative producer, curator, and project manager with a focus on the intersection of art and technology. Stratford co-authored the 2018 methodology report 'Integrating ICT and Art' for the European IoT Large-Scale Pilots, which explicitly references the pioneering work of E.A.T. He has also been involved with the arts company 'Walk the Plank' and participated in international symposia such as 'No Boundaries' (2015) and 'The Art of Leadership' (2018). While some of his recent professional work is dated to the 21st century, his long-term involvement in strategic roles within the arts/tech sector aligns with a career that explores historical and contemporary models of interdisciplinary collaboration.

results.possibleIdentity: Ken Marsh (Kenneth Marsh) is a video artist, painter, and author who was a prominent figure in the early video art and community media movement in the late 1960s and 1970s. Originally trained as a painter and printmaker at Cooper Union, Marsh became a pioneer of portable video technology (Portapak) after being introduced to it in the late 1960s. In 1970, he co-founded the People's Video Theater (PVT) in New York City with Elliot Glass. PVT was a collective focused on "video mediation," using video to document social protests (including the Black Panthers and Young Lords) and hosting public screenings where subjects could respond to their own recorded images. Marsh is frequently associated with the broader "Experiments in Art and Technology" (E.A.T.) milieu of the era, which fostered collaborations between artists and engineers. In 1971, he moved to Woodstock, New York, where he founded Woodstock Community Video and became a central figure in public access cable television. He authored two influential books on the technical aspects of the medium: "Independent Video" (1974) and "The Way The New Technology Works" (1982). Later in his career, he returned to fine arts, focusing on painting and digital installation, and relocated to Los Angeles.

results.possibleIdentity: Jay Bolton (born c. 1943) is a British-born maritime historian, naval architect, and marine artist who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is noted for his work as a skipper and his involvement in the restoration of historic vessels, most notably the 1877 iron-hulled bark Elissa, which he skippered during its restoration and sea trials in the late 1970s and 1980s. Bolton began his maritime career in the 1960s, serving in the merchant marine and later returning to England in the late 1970s. As an artist, he is described as a 'noted artist' whose marine paintings have been included in specialized exhibitions, such as those organized by the National Maritime Historical Society. His background as a naval architect and maritime consultant aligns with the interdisciplinary nature of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which sought to facilitate collaborations between technical professionals (engineers and architects) and artists during the late 1960s and early 1970s, though a formal membership record in E.A.T. remains obscure.

William Jay Bolton (1816–1884), often referred to as Jay Bolton, was a pioneering 19th-century artist credited with producing the first figural stained glass windows in the United States. While he was not active in the 1960s or 1970s, his historical identity became a subject of significant interest and institutional focus during those decades. His masterworks, particularly the 60 stained glass windows at the Church of St. Ann and the Holy Trinity in Brooklyn (executed 1844–1848), were the focus of major restoration campaigns starting in the late 1960s and 1970s funded by organizations like the New York Landmarks Conservancy and the Andy Warhol Foundation. In art historical and grant reports from the 1970s and 1980s, his name often appears alongside modern experimental art projects, such as '9 Evenings Reconsidered' (associated with E.A.T.), due to contemporaneous grants and exhibition calendars, which may lead to his name being associated with that era in archival records.

results.possibleIdentity: Steve Duffy is a professional songwriter active in the 1960s and 1970s, best known for co-writing the country music standard "She Thinks I Still Care." He wrote the song with Dickey Lee (Royden Dickey Lipscomb), and it was first recorded by George Jones in 1962, becoming a massive hit that spent six weeks at number one on the Billboard country chart. The song has since been recorded by numerous prominent artists, including Elvis Presley (1976), Connie Francis (as "He Thinks I Still Care"), Anne Murray, and Leon Russell. Duffy was under contract as a professional songwriter to Jack Clement's publishing company at the Gulf Coast Studio in Beaumont, Texas, during the early 1960s. His work continued to be prominent through the 1970s, with Elvis Presley notably choosing his composition as a B-side for the single "Moody Blue" in 1977.

Steve Duffy (also known by the pseudonym "krumm") is a British digital artist and poet who has been involved in experimental and technology-based art. He has been a participant in early digital culture communities, such as the "Cybermind" and "Wryting" listservs, which fostered collaborations between artists and thinkers interested in the intersection of digital technology and literature. His work has been featured in experimental journals such as Hyperrhiz and the 2River Chapbook Series. In scholarly discussions regarding the history of technology-based art, his name has been cited alongside figures like Billy Klüver (the founder of Experiments in Art and Technology, or E.A.T.) and John Cage, reflecting his association with the avant-garde tradition of combining electronics, digital media, and performance.
